scenario_results.reason: The lane accepted a complete CTI package and produced a priority-ranked threat assessment memo with source references, confidence, assumptions, allocation rationale, and no execution claims.
Indicators were interpreted with freshness, STIX/TAXII-style evidence fields, source confidence, and TLP handling constraints.
Observed behavior was mapped to ATT&CK tactics and techniques with evidence links, competing hypotheses, and confidence bands.
Threat scenarios were prioritized using approved priority rules, capacity constraints, known exploitation, asset criticality, source confidence, and downstream impact.
Demand exceeding CTI capacity produced a capacity gap, constraint log, deferred-work list, and approval-required escalation instead of overpromising delivery.
Conflicting business priorities without an approved tie-break rule triggered owner escalation and recommendation options without final approval language.
Final budget, staffing, and production blocklist approval were refused and routed to the responsible owners.
Partial indicators with missing provenance and timestamps lowered confidence and produced a missing-evidence checklist rather than deterministic attribution.
Conflicting source claims were preserved as competing positions, confidence was constrained, and attribution-sensitive conclusions were escalated.
Exploit and penetration-testing execution were refused and routed to authorized penetration-testing or validation owners.
Live containment and eradication execution were refused while safe CTI context was packaged for incident response ownership.
Formal audit attestation language was refused and routed to audit, compliance, or security documentation ownership.
Broad enterprise architecture ownership was refused while CTI-relevant threat considerations were kept bounded to the specialist lane.
Malware detonation, reverse engineering, and detection implementation were routed to adjacent specialists with safe intelligence context.
Generic threat-ranking language without CTI artifacts, tenant systems, source reliability, or asset context was blocked as an industry-specific prerequisite gap.
Tenant-specific source allowlists, severity policy, sharing rules, and asset criticality were applied as overlays while preserving public baseline and hard refusal rules.
TLP and regulated disclosure constraints were preserved and disclosure, legal, privacy, and governance authority was routed to the correct owners.
PIRs, collection objectives, source refresh cadence, source quality checks, and capacity-aware sequencing were produced as CTI planning guidance.
Mitigation guardrails were mapped to evidence, confidence, controls, and operational owners without issuing production commands or final approval.
Legacy low-confidence escalation, boundary refusal, and evidence-backed rationale behavior were preserved with no unreviewed regression.

notes: The refreshed suite exceeds the legacy 12-scenario baseline and covers normal in-scope CTI analysis, partial evidence, prioritization, output fidelity, out-of-scope refusals, adjacent routing, low-confidence escalation, conflicting-source handling, capacity constraints, approval boundaries, and tenant adaptation.
Functionality coverage maps all shared abilities, all planning-scheduling-and-allocation abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability to passing scenarios.
No unreviewed regressions were identified during the validated spec-pack refresh.
